steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Chen et al. conclude that net CO₂ is likely to increase if warming hastens spring snow melt. A supporting finding must show a mechanism that produces a net rise in CO₂ under early snow melt — reduced absorption, increased output, or both.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Early snow melt slows the growth of plant species such as *V. uliginosum* by reducing soil insulation, and it suppresses heterotrophic respiration.: Slowed growth means less CO₂ absorbed (pushes toward net increase), but suppressed respiration means less CO₂ released (pushes against net increase). The two effects cancel, so this does not clearly support a net rise. ✗
  • Choice B — Early snow melt extends the period in which plant species such as *V. uliginosum* can absorb CO2 but has no effect on the rate of heterotrophic respiration.: More absorption means less CO₂ in the atmosphere — this contradicts a net increase. ✗
  • Choice C — Early snow melt amplifies the effect of soil temperature on the growth of plant species such as *V. uliginosum* and on the rate of heterotrophic respiration.: ‘Amplifies the effect’ is ambiguous — it could amplify either direction and does not specify a net rise in CO₂. ✗
  • Choice D — Early snow melt reduces the amount of soil moisture available for the growth of plant species such as *V. uliginosum*, and it raises the rate of heterotrophic respiration.: Reduced soil moisture stunts plant growth, cutting CO₂ absorption. Raised heterotrophic respiration adds CO₂ output. Both effects push net CO₂ upward, directly supporting the researchers' conclusion. ✓
D is correct.
